speaker: interviewer
text: OK, Toni.

speaker: interviewer
text: September 17, 1996. The interviewee is General Felix Sparks. Interviewer, Toni Katsh Binstock. The interview is being done in Denver, Colorado, USA. And it's being done in English. My name is Toni Katsh Binstock. It's September 17, 1996. Today I am honored to be interviewing General Felix Sparks. The interview is being conducted in Denver, Colorado, United States of America and is being done in English. Would you give me your name and the spelling, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Felix L. Sparks. First name is F-E-L-I-X, L, the middle initial. Last name is Sparks, S-P-A-R-K-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you been known by any other names or nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: My middle name is Laurence, so I'm usually referred to by my middle, my middle name. Most people call me Larry.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your birthdate, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: August 2, 1917.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your age today?

speaker: interviewer
text: And would you give me the city and the spelling of the city in where you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was born in San Antonio, Texas. S-A-N-- it's two words-- S-A-N. Second word is A-N-T-O-N-I-O, Texas.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's United States of America.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your address?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I do not.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a little young at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell about your growing up. Where did you grow up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when I was about four years old-- we lived in Texas until I was about four years old. And my family moved to Arizona. And I actually grew up in Arizona. I went to school there, from grammar school to college in Arizona.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the town or city where you lived?

speaker: interviewee
text: I lived In Miami, Arizona, which is a mining town, copper mining town about 80 miles east of Phoenix, Arizona.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell about your, your family, your father and what he did and your mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my father worked for the mining company, Inspiration Copper Company. My mother never worked. When I was growing up, mothers did not work. My father worked most of his life with Inspiration Copper Company.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many brothers and sisters do you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have one, one brother and three sisters, all younger than I am.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were the oldest?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm the oldest.

speaker: interviewer
text: And would you give us their names, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my brother's name is Earl Sparks. My sister, oldest sister, Ladelle Sparks. Next is Frances Sparks. And the youngest one was Margaret Sparks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the community like in which you lived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was a mining community. And mining communities are-- some people think they're a little rough. I thought it was perfectly normal, myself. But it-- the entire economy depended upon the copper mines. There was no other industry of any kind in that area. So your whole livelihood was based upon the copper mines.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the names of the schools that you attended?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I attended-- the grammar school that I attended, attended was George Washington Grammar School in Miami, Arizona. It was an eight-grade school. After I graduated from there, I attended the Miami High School for four years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family attend church?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we attended the First Baptist Church in Miami, Arizona.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what denomination?

speaker: interviewee
text: Baptist.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know much about Jews or Judaism growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any Jewish people in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, there were a few but very few. There was no synagogue. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I doubt if there were more than half a dozen Jewish people. Of course, when you're growing up, you don't pay much attention to that. But I don't recall any Jewish people at all, to tell you the truth. But I would not have paid any attention had I known.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware of any prejudice against blacks or Jews or anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were prejudice that I experienced was against blacks and against Mexicans. Arizona's on the-- borders [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the Republic of Mexico. So we had a great deal, a great many people who migrated into Arizona from Mexico. And there was considerable discrimination practiced against them. We only had two or three black families. And nobody paid much attention to them. But they were discriminated against in that there was one small school for blacks, in which there were only two or three students. And they attended grammar school in a one-room schoolhouse. However, when they graduated from that, they could then could go to the regular high school. And I only knew one black student all my time in high school. A girl was in there. There was one black girl in my class. And she was the only black in the high school.

speaker: interviewer
text: And if there were Jews, you weren't aware?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't aware of them, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you and your family involved any community organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were your hobbies growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I did a lot of fishing and hunting. And we were in a mountainous country, and I wandered around, climbed mountains, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] played a lot of baseball as kids. And there wasn't a lot to do. We didn't have any money, for one thing, so all the kids pretty well had to entertain themselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after high school, then, did you attend college?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. When I was in high school, at the end of my freshman year, the mines closed down. The Great Depression of the 1930s had started. So the economy throughout the United States suddenly collapsed for no good reason. And the mines closed. My father was out of work. Well, we got by. My father finally got back on with the company after they had laid him off for a while. And he, I think, got about $80 a month as a, as a, as a watchman in one of the mines. So when I graduated from high school, my-- I didn't have any, any way to go to college. I had no money. So I joined the Army [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for a three-year enlistment.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in 1936.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your goals and aspirations before you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I wanted to go to college. I wanted to be a lawyer. But there was no way. I didn't have a dime. No way I could do it. So I figured if I could join the Army for three years and I could save my money, I could start college. And that's exactly what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell about those years in the military.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when I went in the Army in 1936, the Army was extremely small. Not much attention had been made, paid to the military since the days of World War I. So the Army was quite small. And I was sent to Hawaii as a member of the artillery regiment at Pearl Harbor. We were part of the harbor defenses at Pearl Harbor. Military training was rather harsh, and the pay wasn't much. As a private, we got $21 a month. But I got promoted pretty rapidly. I worked hard. And I really enjoyed the, the years that I spent in the Army in Hawaii. And at least it gave me the opportunity to save up enough money to make a, a start in college. And it didn't take much money in those days to go to college.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were in the Army from '36 to '39, the three years?

speaker: interviewee
text: To '38. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In Hawaii, it was considered foreign service in those days. So if you signed up for foreign service, you could get out of the Army in a little over two years. So I only spent a little less than two and a half years in the Army. And then I got out in time to start college in the fall of 1938.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you attend college?

speaker: interviewee
text: University of Arizona at Tucson, Arizona.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did, did you get married at that time or were you still in school, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I started school. And, and I went two and a half years [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when I got a notice that I had been called back into the Army. In the meantime, I had secured a commission as a Second Lieutenant Infantry through a combination of ROTC and what was called a Citizens' Military Training Camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I got a commission as a Second Lieutenant. And in December of 1940, I received a notice to report to the Army for a period of one year. The draft had then started. So they were trying to fill out the Army with draftees and with reserve officers. I was then a reserve officer. So I went back into the Army then in January of 1941. And tell about that-- your career in the military at that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was assigned-- I would've had to report to Fort Huachuca, Arizona, which is down on the Mexican border. And from there I was assigned to the 45th Infantry Division, which was a National Guard Division, which had been mobilized in September of 1940. But they were considerably short of personnel. So I was assigned to the 45th Infantry Division while I was at Fort Sill, Oklahoma. And, and specifically, I was assigned to the 157th Infantry Regiment, which is one of the regiments of the 45th Division. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that was the beginning of that one-year assignment, presumably. Turned out to be over five years. Because before my year was up, Pearl Harbor came along. And of course, then the general mobilization started. And so I spent five years with that regiment.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you when Pearl Harbor--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a student at the Infantry School at Fort Benning, Georgia. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This was a school conducted by the Army for infantry officers. It was-- ran about three months, four months. And I happened to be a student there at the time of Pearl Harbor. And my year was going to be up the following month. So I thought I was going to be getting out of the Army. Instead, what I got-- I remember that Sunday morning. We got the news by radio-- that was before TV-- that the Japs had hit Pearl Harbor. And we all knew that we weren't going to get out in a year.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we geared up, then, for wartime service. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I returned to my unit, which was on Camp Barkelet, Texas. And from there on, we went to various places in the United States and were finally sent to North Africa in June of 1943. At that time, I was a captain. Well, before we go into the wartime experience, tell about-- were you-- you were married sometime between--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I met my wife. She started the University of Arizona the same time I did, so we were in the same freshman class. At that time, the university was fairly small. I mean, I think we had about 800 freshmen students. Today it's about 8,000. But at that time, it was a fairly small university. We met, and we started going together, and-- until I got called into the Army. Then after I got called in the Army, I got to thinking about it. And I missed her. So I called her one day from Abilene, Texas, where I was stationed and said, let's get married. She agreed. I said OK, you, you work out the details. And I'll get a leave. And I'll come to Tucson, Arizona, and we'll get married. Well, that was in June of '41. That was before Pearl Harbor. So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we didn't know at the time we were-- when we got married that I was going to have to spend another over four years in the Army. Anyway, we were married, been married ever since.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she follow you to different camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, she did. She went with me to the various camps. We were stationed in Massachusetts and New York and then Virginia. And from Virginia then we went overseas, and she came home.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were any children born before you went overseas?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, our first child was born while we were in a shipping area in, in Virginia, in Norfolk, Virginia. So our first child was born at that time. And I never, of course, never saw the child. The child was born in Arizona. And the child was two and a half years old before I ever saw him. It was a boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kirk, Kirk.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was, was he born?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was born in 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you were going to tell us about your wartime experiences. Were there any Jews in your unit, in your command at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. There were considerable number of Jewish people in the Army at that time and quite a number in our regiment and division. Matter of fact, at division headquarters there was a Jewish rabbi.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was there any discrimination--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in the service at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a kind of a grim period for all of us. We didn't have time to discriminate. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We depended on each other. When you're in combat, it's-- you have a team. And each team member has to do his part. So there was remarkably little discrimination, if any, that I could determine. We had Mexican boys. We had some Indians. We did not have any blacks in our unit. But we had others like Spanish Americans, Indians, Jewish, you name it. And there was no discrimination.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you went overseas, did you have any idea about what was going on in Germany and with the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very little. I, of course, read the papers. But we didn't get much information at that time, the American people, about what was actually happening in the concentration camps or virtually no information.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there anything in the military publications?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. Nothing at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you were going to tell us about when you did go overseas. Where did you go first?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we shipped out of Norfolk, Virginia. I went to North Africa and made a practice landing. The campaign in North Africa had just ended a short time before we got there. So we didn't actually participate in any combat in North Africa. But we made a practice landing, assault landing on the shores of North Africa and practiced for the invasion of Sicily. So we were in North Africa just a short time, a couple of weeks. And then we loaded back up on our ships, and we were one of the assault units that hit the shores of Sicily in July of 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe what that campaign was like.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was a-- we were new to combat, combat, very green. But all the troops performed quite well. And it took us about 30 days to clear the island of German troops. So there were some pretty hard-fought battles. But it did not last long, and our casualties were fairly light. But at least we got a lot of valuable combat experience in that 30-day period until the Germans actually pulled out of Sicily into Italy. We then followed them. We made, then, the invasion of Salerno, which was a very bloody operation. We lost a lot of people. And the Germans were waiting for us at Salerno. Salerno was an area just south of Naples. And we fought there for almost a full week, trying to establish a beachhead in the Salerno area. And finally, we succeeded. From there on then, we pursued the Germans through Italy up, up till, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] near Rome, where the Germans finally established a defensive position. They were not able to penetrate for about six months.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was about what time of year, then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we landed in Salerno in September of 1943. And by about Christmastime or a little before of '43, the Germans had established a very tough defensive line based on Monte Cassino across the Liri Valley, which is just maybe 30 miles or so south of Rome. At that point, our drive bogged down, starting about the 1st of December. Our problem was compounded by a lot of rain. It rains in Italy in the wintertime. And the rivers rose. We had difficulty crossing the rivers. And the steep mountain terrain made it very difficult to attack. So the Germans were well dug in. And we could not make any-- we didn't make any progress after about the 1st of December until the following May. Almost six months we were stymied there until we built up enough strength to finally crack that Gustav Line in, oh, early May of 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you-- what was your rank then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was a captain. I was a rifle company commander. I had Company E of the 157th Infantry. I was wounded in Italy very seriously, an abdominal wound, which got part of my liver. And I was in a hospital for, oh, over a month in North Africa. And I, I was wounded in late October and did not return until December of '43. From there on then, I was a rifle company commander, up until and including the Invasion of Anzio. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] A decision was made to try to take Rome by landing near Rome at a small port town called Anzio. And our division, along with the 3rd Infantry Division and two British divisions, were given a task of making that landing and taking Rome. We landed, but we didn't take Rome, not for several months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell-- how were you wounded?

speaker: interviewee
text: A shell fragment. Most of the casualties during the war were caused by artillery. Artillery is the biggest casualty-producing agent. Now, the obvious reason is when a shell lands a, a reasonable distance from someone, that shell fragments into hundreds of pieces.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one shell may get 10 people, whereas a rifle bullet can only get one at a time. But a shell can get 10 or 15 people at a time. So I would say during the war that probably 75% of all of our casualties and about 75% of all the German casualties were inflicted by artillery and mortars. Mortars have the same effect as artillery. Did you ever-- did your unit ever capture any German prisoners at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we took prisoners. We took the first prisoners in Sicily. We took some in Italy and France. We took a great number of German prisoners.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, finish telling us about what-- where you went after Anzio and Rome.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, after the Anzio campaign, which was a very bitter campaign, we were bogged down there for almost four months. We lost over 100% of our original strength, our regiment did. Whatever unit went in there was over 100% turnover. Out casualties were over 100%, which means that we had new replacements coming in all the time. But finally we made the break out of Anzio and took Rome. That was in May of 1944. Our division was then pulled out of the lines and sent back to Naples in order to make the invasion of southern France. The Normandy invasion took place on June 6, 1944. Two months later, the Allies invaded southern France. At that time, I was a battalion commander and commanded a battalion then, an infantry battalion, when we made the assault on the shores of southern France.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you there, then, on D-Day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was H plus 10, which meant that my, my job was to get ashore 10 minutes after the first wave. We have a-- first wave hits at H-Hour, which was probably about 5:30 in the morning or 6:00. Five minutes later, the second wave. Another five minutes is a third wave. And the orders were that the battalion commanders had to come ashore as rapidly as possible in order to get control of their units. An amphibious operation is a very difficult operation because you're landing on hostile shores. The enemy-- you're facing an enemy that's already dug in, and you're relatively naked because you're getting out of small boats and rushing at them. So the senior commanders have to get ashore rapidly in order to get control of the units and determine how you're going to assault the German positions.

speaker: interviewee
text: So yes, I was-- all the battalion commanders came ashore at 10 minutes after each hour. And I was one of those battalion commanders. I think I was 25 years old at the-- [LAUGHS] at the time. And then describe what, what was that like for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was-- we'd been in combat then for over a year. So we took it in stride. We had planned. We had extensive planning before the invasion. We knew about what we were up against, where we were going to land, and what the obstacles were. So it went off surprisingly well because the Germans had pulled a lot of troops out of southern France because of the Normandy invasion.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were undermanned. The Germans were undermanned. And we had figured somewhere around 20% casualties for the landing. It actually turned out that we had less than 5%, which was very remarkable.

speaker: interviewee
text: Our casualties were very light. But when I think about light casualties, they're not light for the people who got hit or who were killed. But we had less than 5%, which [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to us was a smashing success. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we then-- we drove on inland fairly rapidly. And we started, then, capturing literally thousands and thousands of German troops that we cut off.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to those troops, then? Where did they go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, of course, we rounded up-- the Germans, they had some very poor troops. They even had some Russians fighting that they had got to fight for them. The Russian battalion that I ran into was actually Mongols, what you'd call a Mongol battalion. They didn't want to fight, and they gave up to my battalion right off. So we captured a, a Mongol battalion without, without firing a shot. We just sent them on back to the rear. But the Germans' defenses really started getting tough as we moved further into France and particularly when we hit the Vosges Mountains. It's a very mountainous area between-- runs along the German-French border in northern France. And at that time, then, the fighting became very bitter and very expensive from there on, heavy casualties on both sides throughout the winter of '44 and '45.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to end this tape and continue on the next one.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
